Cycloalkanes can exhibit geometrical isomerism due to restricted rotation around the ring structure.
Alkynes do not have geometrical isomers because the triple bond does not allow for cis-trans configurations due to linear geometry.
Only alkynes do not have geometrical isomers. Cycloalkanes can exhibit such isomerism.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Alkyne. Geometrical isomerism arises from the restricted rotation around a bond, typically a double bond in the case of alkenes, allowing for cis-trans configurations. Alkynes, however, have a linear geometry due to the triple bond, preventing such isomerism. Cycloalkanes can exhibit geometrical isomerism due to the ring structure which restricts rotation. Therefore, both alkenes and cycloalkanes can have geometrical isomers, but alkynes cannot.

questions.text: Which of the following isomers have Mobile proton?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Metamers are isomers that differ in the arrangement of alkyl groups around a functional group, typically an ether or amine. They do not have mobile protons.
Tautomers are isomers that exist in dynamic equilibrium, typically involving keto-enol forms. They have mobile protons that can shift positions, making this the correct answer.
Positional isomers have the same molecular formula but differ in the position of a functional group within the molecule. They do not inherently involve mobile protons.
Geometrical isomers have the same structural formula but differ in the spatial arrangement of groups around a double bond. They do not involve mobile protons.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Tautomers. Tautomers are a type of isomer that can rapidly interconvert, typically involving the relocation of a hydrogen atom and a shift in a double bond. This movement of protons is characteristic of tautomerism, making tautomers the isomers with mobile protons.Metamers, Positional isomers, and Geometrical isomers do not involve such proton mobility. Metamers differ in the connectivity of atoms, Positional isomers differ in the location of functional groups, and Geometrical isomers differ in the spatial arrangement of atoms or groups, none of which involve a mobile proton.

questions.text: Which of the following are functional group isomers?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Acetone (a ketone) and propionaldehyde (an aldehyde) have the same molecular formula C3H6O but different functional groups.
Acetic acid (a carboxylic acid) and methyl formate (an ester) share the molecular formula C2H4O2 but have different functional groups.
Ethanol (an alcohol) and dimethyl ether (an ether) both have the molecular formula C2H6O with different functional groups.
All the given pairs are functional group isomers since they have the same molecular formulas but different functional groups.

questions.explanation: Functional group isomers are compounds that have the same molecular formula but different functional groups. In this question, each pair of compounds is an example of functional group isomers. Acetone and propionaldehyde have the formula C3H6O but differ as a ketone and an aldehyde, respectively. Acetic acid and methyl formate are isomers with the formula C2H4O2, differing as a carboxylic acid and an ester. Lastly, ethanol and dimethyl ether both have the formula C2H6O, differing as an alcohol and an ether. Hence, the correct answer is 'All of these.'

questions.text: Which of the following may have geometrical isomer?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This compound does have the potential for geometrical isomerism due to the presence of two different substituents on adjacent carbon atoms in the ring.
This compound cannot exhibit geometrical isomerism because both chlorine atoms are attached to the same carbon atom, leaving no room for different spatial arrangements.
This compound also cannot exhibit geometrical isomerism as both methyl groups are on the same carbon, similar to the chlorine example above.
1-Butene can exhibit geometrical isomerism (cis-trans isomerism) due to the presence of a double bond between two carbon atoms, with different groups attached to each carbon in the double bond.

questions.explanation: Geometrical isomerism occurs in compounds that have restricted rotation around a bond, commonly a double bond or within a ring structure, with different substituents attached to the atoms involved. Option C, 1,2-Dimethylcyclopropane, can show geometrical isomerism because the cyclopropane ring restricts rotation and allows for different spatial arrangements of the methyl groups. Option A and Option B, 1,1-Dimethylcyclopropane and 1,1-Dichlorocyclopropane, respectively, cannot exhibit geometrical isomerism because both substituents are on the same carbon atom, preventing any variation in spatial arrangement. Option D, 1-Butene, can also exhibit geometrical isomerism (specifically cis-trans isomerism) due to its double bond, which prevents rotation and allows for different spatial configurations of the attached groups.

questions.text: The following have geometrical isomers?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 2-Methyl butane does not have a double bond, which is necessary for geometrical isomerism.
2-pentene can have geometrical isomers (cis and trans) due to the presence of a double bond with different groups attached.
1,2-Dichloro Propane does not have a double bond or cyclic structure necessary for geometrical isomerism.
This is incorrect because 2-pentene does have geometrical isomers.

questions.explanation: Geometrical isomerism occurs in compounds with restricted rotation around a bond, typically a carbon-carbon double bond, where different groups are attached to the carbons involved in the double bond. In this case, 2-pentene can have cis and trans isomers, as it features a double bond with different groups on each carbon atom. 2-Methyl butane lacks any double bond altogether, ruling out the possibility of such isomerism. 1,2-Dichloro Propane also cannot have geometrical isomers as it lacks a double bond and is not cyclic. Therefore, the correct answer is that 2-pentene has geometrical isomers, making it the correct choice.

questions.text: Which of the following give electrophilic substitution reactions?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Alkanes primarily undergo free radical substitution reactions, not electrophilic substitution.
Alkenes typically undergo electrophilic addition reactions due to the presence of a double bond, rather than substitution.
Alkynes can undergo electrophilic substitution reactions, especially in the presence of certain catalysts or under specific conditions.
This is incorrect because alkynes can undergo electrophilic substitution reactions.

questions.explanation: Alkynes are capable of undergoing electrophilic substitution reactions, although they are less common than addition reactions. This is due to the presence of a triple bond, which can interact with electrophiles under certain conditions. Alkanes, on the other hand, typically undergo free radical substitution reactions, while alkenes are more likely to undergo electrophilic addition reactions because of their double bonds. Thus, the correct answer is 'Alkynes'.

questions.text: During halogenations which of the following react slowly with benzene?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Iodine (I2) reacts very slowly with benzene due to its low reactivity compared to other halogens.
Bromine (Br2) reacts with benzene in the presence of a catalyst like iron (Fe), making it more reactive than iodine.
Chlorine (Cl2) reacts more readily with benzene than iodine, especially in the presence of a catalyst like aluminum chloride (AlCl3).
Fluorine (F2) is extremely reactive and reacts explosively with benzene, making it the most reactive of the halogens listed.

questions.explanation: During halogenation, iodine (I2) reacts very slowly with benzene due to its low reactivity when compared to other halogens. This makes I2 the correct answer. Bromine (Br2) and chlorine (Cl2) react more readily with benzene, especially in the presence of catalysts like iron or aluminum chloride, respectively. Fluorine (F2) is highly reactive, even explosively so, with benzene, making it the most reactive halogen among the options. Therefore, I2 is the only halogen that reacts slowly with benzene without the need for special conditions or catalysts.

questions.text: Benzene is oxidized with V2O5 into
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Fumaric acid is an unsaturated dicarboxylic acid, not typically formed from the oxidation of benzene.
Maleic anhydride is the correct product formed when benzene is oxidized with V2O5.
Ethandioic acid, also known as oxalic acid, is not a typical product of benzene oxidation.
Adipic acid is a dicarboxylic acid used in the production of nylon. It is not formed from the oxidation of benzene with V2O5.

questions.explanation: Benzene, when oxidized with V2O5, forms maleic anhydride. This is a common industrial process for producing maleic anhydride from benzene. The other options provided (fumaric acid, ethandioic acid, and adipic acid) are not typical products of benzene oxidation with V2O5. Fumaric acid and ethandioic acid are different types of carboxylic acids, and adipic acid is generally produced from cyclohexane oxidation.

questions.text: The reaction not concern with benzene
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Elimination reactions generally involve the removal of atoms or groups from a molecule to form a double or triple bond, which is not common with benzene due to its stable aromatic ring structure.
Benzene commonly undergoes substitution reactions, where one of its hydrogen atoms is replaced by another atom or group, preserving its aromatic stability.
Addition reactions are rare for benzene because they would disrupt the aromatic stability of the benzene ring, although they can occur under extreme conditions.
While benzene itself is resistant to oxidation under normal conditions, it can be oxidized under specific conditions, particularly at the side chains.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Elimination. Benzene is characterized by its stable aromatic ring structure, which is not conducive to elimination reactions. Elimination reactions typically involve the formation of double or triple bonds, which would disrupt the aromatic stability of benzene. In contrast, benzene commonly undergoes substitution reactions, where one atom or group is replaced by another while maintaining its aromatic structure. Addition reactions are less common because they can disrupt the aromatic system, although they are possible under certain conditions. Oxidation of benzene itself is difficult, but its side chains can be oxidized under specific conditions.

questions.text: Identify the correct statement
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Isomers have the same molecular weight
options.explanation: Isomers have the same molecular weight because they have the same number and type of atoms, but their arrangement is different.

options.text: Isomers have the same chemical formula
options.explanation: Isomers share the same chemical formula, which is why they are classified as isomers, despite having different structural arrangements.

options.text: SO3 acts as the electrophile during the sulphonation of benzene
options.explanation: In the sulphonation of benzene, SO3 is the electrophile that reacts with benzene to form benzenesulfonic acid.
All the statements are correct: Isomers have the same molecular weight and chemical formula, and SO3 is the electrophile during benzene sulphonation.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is All of these because: Isomers have the same molecular weight due to their identical numbers and types of atoms, despite differences in their structural configurations.They share the same chemical formula, which is the definition of isomers.In the context of aromatic sulphonation, SO3 serves as the electrophile that reacts with benzene, confirming its role in the process described. Therefore, each statement presented in the options is correct.

questions.text: The conversion of acetylene into benzene is called.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Isomerization involves converting one molecule into another molecule with the same molecular formula but a different structural arrangement. This is not the process that converts acetylene into benzene.
Rearrangement refers to the reorganization of atoms within a molecule. Although this involves structural changes, it is not the correct process for forming benzene from acetylene.
Polymerization is the process where small molecules, called monomers (such as acetylene), join together to form larger molecules (in this case, benzene). This is the correct process.
This option is incorrect because polymerization is indeed the correct process for converting acetylene into benzene.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Polymerization. This process involves the combination of small molecules, acetylene, to form a larger molecule, benzene, through a series of reactions. The other options are incorrect because:Isomerization: This involves converting a molecule into another molecule with the same molecular formula but a different structure, which does not apply here.Rearrangement: This refers to the reorganization of atoms within a molecule, which is not the process used to form benzene from acetylene.None of these: This is incorrect as polymerization is indeed the correct method.

questions.text: Geometrical isomerization is shown by
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Lactic acid does not have a double bond with different groups on each carbon, which is necessary for geometrical isomerism.
Fumaric acid has a double bond and different groups attached to the carbons involved, allowing for geometrical isomerism.
Tartaric acid lacks the structural requirements for geometrical isomerism, as it does not have the necessary double bond arrangement.
Not all the options can exhibit geometrical isomerism, so this choice is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Geometrical isomerism occurs in compounds with double bonds where each carbon of the double bond has two different substituents. Fumaric acid, which is a dicarboxylic acid, has a double bond in its structure with different groups attached to each carbon of the double bond, allowing for geometrical isomerism. Lactic acid and tartaric acid do not possess the necessary double bond with varying substituents required for this type of isomerism. Therefore, the correct answer is Fumaric acid.

questions.text: When alkyne is reacted with Na & NH3 (Liq), the resultant product is.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Correct. The reaction of an alkyne with sodium and liquid ammonia results in a trans-alkene due to the anti-addition mechanism.
Incorrect. Cis-alkenes are typically formed by partial hydrogenation using a Lindlar catalyst, not by reaction with Na in liquid ammonia.
Incorrect. An alkane is formed by complete hydrogenation of an alkyne, which requires different conditions and reagents.
Incorrect. Not all these products are possible under the given reaction conditions.

questions.explanation: When an alkyne is reacted with sodium (Na) and liquid ammonia (NH3), it undergoes partial reduction to form a trans-alkene. This is because the reaction proceeds via an anti-addition mechanism, where the two hydrogen atoms add to opposite sides of the alkyne, resulting in a trans configuration. The cis-alkene is not formed under these conditions; instead, it is typically produced when using a Lindlar catalyst. An alkane would result from complete hydrogenation, which these reagents do not facilitate. Therefore, the correct product is a trans-alkene.

questions.text: Which of the following is used as dehydrating agent?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Both H2SO4 and P4O10 are strong dehydrating agents, commonly used to remove water from chemical compounds.
Al2O3 acts as a drying agent in some contexts, and H2SO4 is a well-known dehydrating agent.
H3PO4 and P2O5 are both effective dehydrating agents, especially in phosphorus chemistry.
All of the substances listed in the options are known as effective dehydrating agents.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'All of these'. Each of the substances mentioned in the options—H2SO4, P4O10, Al2O3, H3PO4, and P2O5—are recognized as dehydrating agents. H2SO4 is a strong acid that can remove water from compounds, P4O10 is used to dehydrate acids and organic compounds, Al2O3 serves a similar purpose in some industrial processes, and phosphorus-based compounds like H3PO4 and P2O5 are used in dehydrating reactions. Therefore, the correct choice encompasses all these agents.

questions.text: Which of the following procedures is correct, if it is required to synthesize, m-bromonitrobenzene from benzene, bromine, nitric acid and sulphuric acid?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Bromination cannot take place without bromine. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Benzene is nitrated in the presence of sulphuric acid. Hence, this option is incorrect.

options.text: Nitrate the benzene and then brominate
options.explanation: We know that a nitro group is a meta director. So, we first make nitrobenzene, then brominate it to obtain m-bromonitrobenzene. Direct bromination of benzene would place on the ring one bromine atom that would then direct the second bromine atom into the ortho or para position.

options.text: Brominate the benzene and then nitrate
options.explanation: Since the first reaction (bromination) installs an ortho- and para-director, the second reaction (nitration) will result in the undesired para- and ortho-products. Since we want the meta product, the first step should be the installation of a meta-director (nitration) followed by bromination. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: We know that a nitro group is a meta director. So, we first make nitrobenzene, then brominate it to obtain m-bromonitrobenzene. Direct bromination of benzene would place on the ring one bromine atom that would then direct the second bromine atom into the ortho or para position.

questions.text: Ethylene glycol is formed by the reaction of ethylene with:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ethylene reacts with oxygen to form ethylene oxide, which further reacts with water to yield ethylene glycol. This is the correct sequence of reactions.
Hypohalous acids react with ethylene to form halohydrins, not ethylene glycol, as ethylene glycol does not contain any halogen atoms. Therefore, this option is incorrect.
LiAlH4 is a strong reducing agent used to reduce compounds, whereas the formation of ethylene glycol from ethylene requires an oxidation process. Thus, this option is incorrect.
NaBH4 is a mild reducing agent, and similar to LiAlH4, it is used for reduction, not oxidation which is needed to form ethylene glycol. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Oxygen and water'. Ethylene first reacts with oxygen to form ethylene oxide, a reaction that is an example of oxidation. This ethylene oxide then undergoes a hydration reaction with water to form ethylene glycol. Other options involve halogenation or reduction processes that are not relevant to the formation of ethylene glycol from ethylene.

questions.text: Which of the following compounds shows geometrical isomerism?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 1-bromoethylene has the structure CH2=CHBr. The carbon atom with the double bond to bromine has two hydrogen atoms, which are identical, thus it cannot exhibit geometrical isomerism. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

2-methylpropene has the structure (CH3)2C=CH2. The carbon atom with the double bond to the methyl group has two identical groups (methyl), which means it cannot exhibit geometrical isomerism. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

2-methyl-2-butene has the structure CH3C(CH3)=CHCH3. The central carbon in the double bond has two identical methyl groups, which prevents it from having geometrical isomers. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

1-bromo-2-chloropropene has the structure CH(Br)=CH(Cl)-CH3. Each carbon atom in the double bond is bonded to two different groups, allowing for the possibility of geometrical isomerism (cis-trans). Therefore, this option is correct.

questions.explanation: Geometrical isomerism occurs when there is restricted rotation around a double bond and each carbon atom of the double bond is attached to two different groups. In 1-bromo-2-chloropropene, the carbon atoms in the double bond are connected to different groups: one carbon has a bromine and a hydrogen, and the other has a chlorine and a hydrogen, allowing for cis-trans isomerism. In contrast, the other options have at least one carbon atom in the double bond attached to identical groups, which prevents them from exhibiting geometrical isomerism.

questions.text: When benzene reacts with chlorine in the presence of FeCl3, it gives
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The reaction between benzene and chlorine in the presence of FeCl3 results in the formation of chlorobenzene, as FeCl3 acts as a catalyst in electrophilic aromatic substitution.
Benzyl chloride formation requires the presence of a methyl group attached to the benzene ring, which is not the case in this reaction.
Benzyl dichloride would require a methylene group (CH2) on the benzene ring, which is not involved in this reaction.
Benzyl trichloride formation would necessitate a methyl group fully substituted with chlorine atoms, which is not possible here.

questions.explanation: In the presence of FeCl3, chlorine reacts with benzene to form chlorobenzene. This is an electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction, where FeCl3 acts as a Lewis acid catalyst, facilitating the formation of the chloronium ion, which then substitutes a hydrogen atom on the benzene ring. The other options involve side chains on the benzene ring, which are not present in this reaction.

questions.text: Identify the compounds (A-H) that have a conjugated system.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Figure A does not have alternating single and double bonds, hence it lacks a conjugated system.
Figure B contains some alternating single and double bonds, indicating a partially conjugated system.
Figure C has alternating single and double bonds, but they do not form a fully conjugated system.
Figure D is benzene, which has a fully conjugated system with alternating single and double bonds around the ring.
Figure E lacks a pattern of alternating single and double bonds, so it is not conjugated.
Figure F has some alternating single and double bonds but is not fully conjugated like benzene.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Figure D, which represents benzene. Benzene has a fully conjugated system, meaning it has alternating single and double bonds throughout the entire molecule, forming a stable aromatic ring. In contrast, Figures A and E do not have any conjugated systems as they lack alternating bonds. Figures B, C, and F have some portions with alternating bonds, indicating partial conjugation, but they do not represent fully conjugated systems like benzene.

questions.text: The reaction between propanoyl chloride and benzene is an example of acylation. Which is a correct representation of part of the mechanism of this reaction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Figure A does not relate to the mechanism of acylation involving propanoyl chloride and benzene.

Figure B illustrates the formation of an electrophile by the interaction of AlCl3 with propanoyl chloride. While this is a step in the process, it is not the complete depiction of the acylation mechanism.
Figure C accurately depicts the acylation process, showing the formation of a phenonium ion and the attachment of the electrophile to the benzene ring, resulting in a ketonic structure.
Figure D incorrectly suggests that the positive ion in the benzene ring is removed by AlCl4-, overlooking the key step of electrophile attachment.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct because it shows the formation of a phenonium ion and the attachment of the electrophile to the benzene ring, which is the essence of the acylation mechanism, forming a ketonic structure. Option A is unrelated to the mechanism in question. Option B describes the creation of the electrophile but not the complete mechanism. Option D incorrectly describes the removal process of the positive ion in the benzene ring, which is not done by AlCl4- in this context.

questions.text: Which type of compounds cannot exhibit geometrical isomerism?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Singly bonded compounds, also known as saturated compounds, do not have double bonds. Therefore, they cannot exhibit geometrical isomerism.
Doubly bonded compounds, such as alkenes, have a double bond between two carbon atoms. This double bond restricts rotation and allows for the possibility of geometrical isomerism.

Triply Bonded because compounds with triple bonds have a linear structure, and this linear arrangement does not allow for cis-trans isomerism. In a triple bond, the atoms or groups attached to the carbons are in a straight line, and there is no possibility of having different spatial arrangements of groups on the same side or opposite sides of the molecule. Therefore, triple bonded compounds cannot exhibit geometrical isomerism.

Singly bonded compounds, also known as saturated compounds, do not have double bonds. Therefore, they cannot exhibit geometrical isomerism.Triply Bonded because compounds with triple bonds have a linear structure, and this linear arrangement does not allow for cis-trans isomerism. In a triple bond, the atoms or groups attached to the carbons are in a straight line, and there is no possibility of having different spatial arrangements of groups on the same side or opposite sides of the molecule. Therefore, triple bonded compounds cannot exhibit geometrical isomerism.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is (D) Singly bonded compounds, also known as saturated compounds, do not have double bonds. Therefore, they cannot exhibit geometrical isomerism. Triply Bonded because compounds with triple bonds have a linear structure, and this linear arrangement does not allow for cis-trans isomerism. In a triple bond, the atoms or groups attached to the carbons are in a straight line, and there is no possibility of having different spatial arrangements of groups on the same side or opposite sides of the molecule. Therefore, triple bonded compounds cannot exhibit geometrical isomerism.

questions.text: How many chain isomers could be obtained from the alkane C6H14?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: There are more than four chain isomers for hexane (C6H14), so this option is incorrect.
There are exactly five distinct chain isomers possible for hexane (C6H14).
Although there are many structural possibilities, only five of them result in unique chain isomers for hexane.
Seven chain isomers exceed the actual number possible for hexane.

questions.explanation: Hexane (C6H14) can form five distinct chain isomers due to different ways of branching the carbon chain. These isomers include n-hexane, 2-methylpentane, 3-methylpentane, 2,2-dimethylbutane, and 2,3-dimethylbutane. Each represents a unique carbon skeleton. Options stating four, six, or seven chain isomers are incorrect because they either underestimate or overestimate the possible unique structures.

questions.text: The number of isomers in C4H10O will be:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: There are a total of seven isomers for C₄H₁₀O, including both alcohols and ethers. These isomers include 4 alcohols and 3 ethers.
This option is incorrect because there are no additional isomers beyond the seven known for C₄H₁₀O.
This number is too low. It does not account for all possible structural arrangements of alcohols and ethers with the formula C₄H₁₀O.
While close, this number is still incorrect as it does not include all possible isomers of C₄H₁₀O.

questions.explanation: To determine the number of isomers for C₄H₁₀O, we must consider both alcohols (where the oxygen is bonded to a carbon and hydrogen) and ethers (where the oxygen is bonded to two carbons). There are 4 possible alcohol isomers and 3 ether isomers, making a total of 7 isomers.Option A is correct because it accounts for all possible structural variations. Options B, C, and D are incorrect as they underestimate or overestimate the number of possible isomers by failing to account for all structural permutations.

questions.text: Which one of the following is most reactive towards electrophilic reagent?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option lacks electron-donating groups that significantly increase reactivity towards electrophiles.
Option B is correct because the +R (resonance) effect of the -OH group is stronger than that of the -OCH3 group, making it more reactive towards electrophiles.
This option may contain groups with electron-withdrawing effects, which decrease reactivity towards electrophiles.
This option does not have substituents that contribute significantly to electrophilic reactivity.

questions.explanation: Option B is the most reactive towards electrophilic reagents due to the strong +R effect of the -OH group, which donates electron density into the aromatic ring, enhancing its reactivity. In comparison, the -OCH3 group in other options, while also donating electrons, does not provide as strong an effect as the -OH group. Options A, C, and D either lack such electron-donating groups or have groups with weaker electron-donating capabilities, making them less reactive towards electrophiles.

questions.text: Samples of the gases CH3Cl and Cl2 are mixed together and irradiated with light. Which compound is produced in trace amounts by a termination stage in the chain reaction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: HCl is formed during the propagation step when a chlorine radical abstracts a hydrogen from methane, not during termination.
Ethene (CH2=CH2) is not a product of this reaction. It involves carbon-carbon double bonds, which are not formed in this mechanism.
Dichloromethane can be formed in further propagation steps, but it is not a termination product.
Ethane (C2H6) is formed in trace amounts during the termination step when two methyl radicals combine.

questions.explanation: In the radical chain reaction initiated by UV light, the initiation step generates chlorine radicals. The propagation steps involve the abstraction of hydrogen atoms, forming HCl and methyl radicals. During the termination step, two radicals combine to form a stable molecule. One termination reaction is the combination of two methyl radicals to form ethane (CH3CH3), which is produced in trace amounts. Other options such as HCl, ethene, and dichloromethane are either products of propagation or are not involved in this mechanism.

questions.text: Which of the following is a incorrect statement about the two alkenes CH3CH2CH=CH2 and CH3CH=CHCH3?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: 1-butene doesn't exhibit cis-trans (geometrical) isomerism while 2-butene does.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. 1-butene cannot exhibit cis-trans isomerism because it has identical hydrogen atoms attached to one of the double-bonded carbon atoms, whereas 2-butene can show geometrical isomerism due to different groups attached to the double-bonded carbons.

options.text: Neither of the alkenes can be polymerized.
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Both 1-butene and 2-butene can undergo polymerization to form polymers, a characteristic reaction of alkenes.

options.text: Both alkenes can react with hydrogen to form butane.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Both 1-butene and 2-butene can react with hydrogen gas in a hydrogenation reaction to form butane, converting the double bond into a single bond.
This option is incorrect because there is at least one incorrect statement among the provided options.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B: Neither of the alkenes can be polymerized. This statement is incorrect because both 1-butene and 2-butene are capable of polymerization. Option A is correct as 1-butene does not exhibit geometrical isomerism due to identical groups on one of the double-bonded carbon atoms, unlike 2-butene. Option D is correct, as both alkenes can undergo hydrogenation to form butane. Option C, 'None of these,' is incorrect as there is a false statement among the options.

questions.text: Which one is most reactive towards nucleophilic addition reaction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is a simple alkyl aldehyde, which is generally reactive due to the carbonyl group, but lacks additional activating substituents.
Option B is an alkyl ketone, which is less reactive towards nucleophilic addition compared to aldehydes due to steric hindrance and less electrophilic carbonyl carbon.
Option C is an aromatic ketone with an electron donating group (-CH3), which decreases the reactivity towards nucleophilic addition reactions.

Option D is an aromatic aldehyde with an electron withdrawing group (-NO2), increasing its reactivity towards nucleophilic addition reactions due to the enhanced electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D. Aromatic aldehydes are generally more reactive than ketones in nucleophilic addition reactions due to less steric hindrance and greater electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon. Furthermore, the presence of an electron withdrawing group like -NO2 in aromatic aldehydes significantly increases their reactivity by making the carbonyl carbon more electrophilic. In contrast, electron donating groups such as -CH3 in aromatic ketones reduce reactivity by decreasing the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon. Therefore, aromatic aldehydes with electron withdrawing groups are the most reactive.

questions.text: Which of the following organic compounds does not show geometric isomerism?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 2-Butene can exhibit geometric isomerism because the carbon atoms involved in the double bond have different groups attached, allowing for cis and trans configurations.
3-Hexene can also exhibit geometric isomerism because the double-bonded carbon atoms have different substituents, enabling cis and trans isomers.
2-Pentene shows geometric isomerism as the carbon atoms in the double bond have different groups, allowing for the formation of cis and trans isomers.
1-Butene does not show geometric isomerism because one of the double-bonded carbons has two identical hydrogen atoms attached, preventing the formation of distinct cis or trans configurations.

questions.explanation: For geometric (cis-trans) isomerism to exist, each carbon atom of the double bond must be attached to two different groups. In 1-butene, the first carbon of the double bond is attached to two identical hydrogen atoms, making geometric isomerism impossible.

questions.text: Which of the following compounds does not exhibit positional isomerism
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Alkynes can exhibit positional isomerism by varying the position of the triple bond along the carbon chain.
Nitroalkanes can show positional isomerism depending on the location of the nitro group in the carbon chain.
Carboxylic acids have a fixed position for the carboxyl group at the end of the carbon chain, so they do not exhibit positional isomerism.
Alcohols can exhibit positional isomerism by altering the position of the hydroxyl group on the carbon chain.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Carboxylic acid. The carboxyl group (-COOH) in carboxylic acids is always located at the terminal end of the carbon chain, which leaves no room for positional variation, hence no positional isomerism. In contrast, alkynes can exhibit positional isomerism by changing the position of the triple bond, nitroalkanes can do so by relocating the nitro group, and alcohols can vary the position of the hydroxyl group, all allowing for positional isomerism.

questions.text: Total number of possible chain isomers of butylalcohol among alcohols are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Correct. The four chain isomers of butyl alcohol are n-butanol, isobutanol, sec-butanol, and tert-butanol.
Incorrect. There are only four possible chain isomers of butyl alcohol. The fifth structure would be a repetition of one of the existing isomers.
Incorrect. Three isomers do not account for all possible variations; one isomer is missing from this count.
Incorrect. Six isomers exceed the number of possible structures for butyl alcohol, leading to redundant or invalid structures.

questions.explanation: Butanol (C₂H₂OH) has four structural isomers. These are butan-1-ol, butan-2-ol, 2-methylpropan-1-ol (isobutanol), and 2-methylpropan-2-ol (tert-butanol), representing different arrangements of the carbon chain and positions of the hydroxyl group.

questions.text: The type of isomerism shown in the following species is
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Functional group isomerism occurs when compounds have the same molecular formula but different functional groups.
Tautomerism involves the transfer of a proton and a shift in the position of a double bond, typically seen in keto-enol forms.
Metamerism occurs in compounds with the same molecular formula but different alkyl groups on either side of a functional group.
Cis-trans isomerism is a type of stereoisomerism where atoms are positioned differently around a double bond or a ring structure.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is tautomerism. Tautomerism is a specific type of isomerism where a proton shifts within the molecule, often accompanied by a change in the position of a double bond. This is commonly observed in keto-enol tautomers. In contrast, functional group isomerism involves different functional groups, metamerism is about varying alkyl chains, and cis-trans isomerism deals with spatial arrangement around double bonds or rings.

questions.text: Choose the correct name according to IUPAC nomenclature
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This name suggests that there are two ethyl groups and one methyl group attached to the pentane chain. However, the placement of the ethyl and methyl groups is incorrect based on the given structure.

According to IUPAC nomenclature Number the longest chain, beginning with the end nearest a branch. When two or more different substituents are present, list them in alphabetical order. This name suggests that there is one ethyl group at the 3rd carbon and one methyl group at the 2nd carbon of the pentane chain. The placement of the substituents is consistent with the name.

This name suggests that there is one methyl group attached to the cyclohexane ring. However, the numbering of the carbon atoms in cyclohexane should start with the substituent that has the highest priority. In this case, the numbering should start with the methyl group. Therefore, the correct name would be 1-methyl cyclohexane.

This name suggests that there is one ethyl group at the 3rd carbon and one methyl group at the 4th carbon of the pentane chain. However, based on the given structure, the placement of the substituents is inconsistent.

questions.explanation: According to IUPAC nomenclature Number the longest chain, beginning with the end nearest a branch. When two or more different substituents are present, list them in alphabetical order. This name suggests that there is one ethyl group at the 3rd carbon and one methyl group at the 2nd carbon of the pentane chain. The placement of the substituents is consistent with the name, making this option A correct choice. While other options don't follow the IUPAC nomenclature according to the given figure.

questions.text: What is the IUPAC name of this structure
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option incorrectly identifies the substituents and their positions on the main chain.
This option uses an incorrect naming format, as 'Iso' should not be used as a prefix in IUPAC naming, and it also lacks information about all substituents and their positions.
This option incorrectly identifies the substituents and their positions, missing the presence of an isopropyl group.
This is the correct IUPAC name. The main chain is hexane, with an isopropyl and a methyl group both attached to the third carbon.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 3-Isopropyl-3-methyl hexane. The longest continuous carbon chain has six carbons, making it a hexane. The substituents are an isopropyl group and a methyl group, both attached to the third carbon of the chain, hence 3-isopropyl-3-methyl. Option A and C incorrectly identify and place the substituents, while Option B uses an incorrect naming format and lacks complete substituent details.

questions.text: Which of the following is structure of isobutyl radical?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is butyl, a straight chain (normal butyl), not isobutyl
This is sec-butyl, not isobutyl

The isobutyl radical is derived from isobutane (2-methylpropane) by removing one hydrogen atom. The molecular formula of isobutyl radical is C4H9. Its structure can be represented as follows:CH3-CH-CH3-CH2-CH3This structure reflects the arrangement of atoms in the isobutyl radical, where there is a branched chain with a methyl group CH3 attached to the second carbon atom. Therefore this option is correct.
This is tert-butyl, not isobutyl

questions.explanation: The isobutyl radical is derived from isobutane (2-methylpropane) by removing one hydrogen atom. The molecular formula of isobutyl radical is C4H9. Its structure can be represented as follows:CH3-CH-CH3-CH2-CH3This structure reflects the arrangement of atoms in the isobutyl radical, where there is a branched chain with a methyl group CH3 attached to the second carbon atom. Therefore option C is correct.

questions.text: Which of the following hydrocarbons is least reactive?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Alkynes have a triple bond composed of one sigma and two pi bonds. While the pi bonds make alkynes reactive, they are more stable than alkenes but less stable than alkanes and benzene due to the shorter bond length and less exposed pi bonds compared to alkenes.
Alkanes only contain single sigma bonds between carbon atoms, which are strong and stable. This makes alkanes the least reactive among hydrocarbons.
Benzene has a stable ring structure with delocalized pi electrons, making it less reactive than alkenes and alkynes but more reactive than alkanes due to the resonance-stabilized pi bonds.
Alkenes contain a double bond, consisting of one sigma and one pi bond. The pi bond is weaker and more exposed, making alkenes more reactive compared to alkanes, benzene, and alkynes.

questions.explanation: Alkanes are saturated hydrocarbons containing only strong, non-polar carbon-carbon and carbon-hydrogen single bonds (sigma bonds). The absence of weaker pi bonds or functional groups makes them relatively inert and the least reactive class of hydrocarbons.

questions.text: Which of the following is the most stable free radical?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A depicts a tertiary radical, which is the most stable free radical due to hyperconjugation, inductive effect, and resonance stabilization provided by neighboring carbon atoms. Tertiary radicals are more stable than primary, secondary, or methyl radicals due to the distribution of the radical electron over multiple carbon atoms. Therefore, this option is correct.

Option B represents a primary radical, which is less stable than tertiary radicals due to factors like lack of hyperconjugation, weaker inductive effect, and greater exposure of the radical site. Primary radicals are generally more reactive and less stable compared to tertiary radicals. Hence, this option is not correct.

Option C illustrates a secondary radical, which falls between primary and tertiary radicals in terms of stability. While secondary radicals have moderate hyperconjugation and inductive effects, they are less stable than tertiary radicals. This option is not correct.

Option D showcases a methyl radical, which is the least stable due to the absence of hyperconjugation, weak inductive effect, and high exposure of the radical site. Methyl radicals are highly reactive and less stable compared to radicals with more substituted carbon atoms. Therefore, this option is not correct.

questions.explanation: Option A depicts a tertiary radical, which is the most stable free radical among the given options. Tertiary radicals benefit from hyperconjugation, inductive effect, and resonance stabilization, making them more stable than primary, secondary, or methyl radicals. The stability of tertiary radicals is attributed to the distribution of the radical electron over multiple carbon atoms. Hence, option A is correct.Options B, C, and D represent primary, secondary, and methyl radicals, respectively, which are less stable than tertiary radicals. These radicals lack the stabilizing effects of hyperconjugation, inductive effect, and resonance stabilization seen in tertiary radicals. Therefore, options B, C, and D are not correct.

questions.text: Which of the following is the least stable carbocation?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is a tertiary carbocation with three alkyl groups, making it the most stable due to the maximum hyperconjugation and inductive effects.
This is a methyl carbocation with no alkyl groups attached, making it the least stable due to lack of electron-donating groups.
This is a secondary carbocation with two alkyl groups, providing moderate stability.
This is a primary carbocation with one alkyl group, offering some stability but less than secondary or tertiary.

questions.explanation: The stability of carbocations is increased by electron-donating groups, such as alkyl groups, through inductive effects and hyperconjugation. The methyl carbocation (CH₃+) has no alkyl groups attached and is therefore the least stable. The order of stability is tertiary > secondary > primary > methyl.

questions.text: Which of the following pair of organic compounds shows position isomerism?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: No, CH₃-CH₂-CHO (propanal) and CH₃-CO-CH₃ (propane-2-one or acetone) do not exhibit position isomerism. Position isomerism involves the variation in the position of functional groups or substituents along the carbon chain of a molecule. In propanal (CH₃-CH₂-CHO), the aldehyde functional group (-CHO) is located at the end of the carbon chain. In propane-2-one or acetone (CH₃-CO-CH₃), the ketone functional group (C=O) is situated in the middle of the carbon chain. Since the functional groups are different (aldehyde in propanal, ketone in acetone) and are not substituents along a carbon chain, these compounds do not demonstrate position isomerism. They are better described as functional group isomers or constitutional isomers. Therefore this option is not correct.

Yes, CH₃-CH₂-CH₂-OH (1-propanol) and CH₃-CH(OH)CH₃ (2-propanol or isopropanol) are examples of position isomers. They have the same molecular formula, C₃H₈O, but differ in the position of the hydroxyl (OH) group along the carbon chain.In 1-propanol, the hydroxyl group is attached to the terminal carbon of the three-carbon chain:CH₃-CH₂-CH₂-OHIn 2-propanol or isopropanol, the hydroxyl group is attached to the middle carbon of the three-carbon chain: CH₃-CH(OH)CH₃. The variation in the position of the hydroxyl group in these isomers illustrates position isomerism.

The compounds C₂H₅-O-C₂H₅ (diethyl ether) and CH₃-O-C₃H₇ (methyl propyl ether or isopropyl methyl ether) are not position isomers. They are indeed constitutional isomers (or structural isomers) since they have the same molecular formula (C₄H₁₀O) but differ in the arrangement of their atoms. Hence this option is not correct.

You are correct; CH₃-COOH (methanoic acid) and HCOOCH₃ (methyl formate) are not position isomers. They are, in fact, functional group isomers.1. **CH₃-COOH (methanoic acid):**- This compound is a carboxylic acid with the functional group -COOH.2. **HCOOCH₃ (methyl formate):**- This compound is an ester with the functional group -COOCH₃.While they both have the same molecular formula, HCOOH, and the carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen atoms are connected differently, resulting in distinct functional groups. Hence this option is not correct.

questions.explanation: Yes, CH₃-CH₂-CH₂-OH (1-propanol) and CH₃-CH(OH)CH₃ (2-propanol or isopropanol) are examples of position isomers. They have the same molecular formula, C₃H₈O, but differ in the position of the hydroxyl (OH) group along the carbon chain.In 1-propanol, the hydroxyl group is attached to the terminal carbon of the three-carbon chain:CH₃-CH₂-CH₂-OHIn 2-propanol or isopropanol, the hydroxyl group is attached to the middle carbon of the three-carbon chain: CH₃-CH(OH)CH₃. The variation in the position of the hydroxyl group in these isomers illustrates position isomerism. .Hence option B is correct.

questions.text: Acetaldehyde and ethenol show _
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Stereoisomerism is a form of isomerim in which molecules have the same molecular formula but sequence of bonded atoms (constitution),but different in three dimensional orientations of their atoms in space.
Metamerism is a form of isomerism arises due to unequal distribution of carbon atoms on either side of the functional group.
Position isomerism arises due to the difference in the position of the same functional group on the carbon chain.The arrangement of carbon atoms remain same.
Acetaldehyde and ethanol shows tautomerism.This type of isomerism arises due to shifting of proton from one atom to other in the same molecule

questions.explanation: Tautomerism Acetaldehyde and ethanol shows tautomerism.This type of isomerism arises due to shifting of proton from one atom to other in the same molecule Stereoisomerism is a form of isomerim in which molecules have the same molecular formula but sequence of bonded atoms (constitution),but different in three dimensional orientations of their atoms in space. Metamerism is a form of isomerism arises due to unequal distribution of carbon atoms on either side of the functional group. Position isomerism arises due to the difference in the position of the same functional group on the carbon chain.The arrangement of carbon atoms remain same. Reference:Punjab textbook

questions.text: How many alcohols are structural isomers with the formula: C5H11OH?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option overlooks several structural possibilities. Explore different carbon skeletons and hydroxyl locations for a complete count.
This estimate is too low. Ensure you consider all branching variations and hydroxyl placements.
Close, but there is one more isomer. Double-check all possible configurations of the carbon chain and hydroxyl group.
Correct! All possible structural isomers, including different carbon branchings and hydroxyl placements, are accounted for.

questions.explanation: The structural isomers of alcohols with the formula C5H11OH are determined by exploring all possible configurations of the carbon skeleton and the hydroxyl group (-OH) position. The eight distinct isomers include:1-Pentanol2-Pentanol3-Pentanol2-Methyl-1-butanol2-Methyl-2-butanol3-Methyl-1-butanol3-Methyl-2-butanol2,2-Dimethyl-1-propanolTherefore, Option D is correct. Options A, B, and C underestimate the number of isomers by not considering all potential configurations of both the carbon chain and the hydroxyl group.

questions.text: Which of the following does not show geometrical isomerism?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This compound has a double bond with different groups attached to the double-bonded carbons, allowing geometrical isomerism.
The double bond in this compound is flanked by carbons with different substituents, supporting geometical isomerism.
This compound cannot show geometrical isomerism because the double-bonded carbon is attached to identical groups (chlorine), preventing variation in spatial arrangement.
Though the chlorine atoms are at the ends, the central double bond has different substituents, allowing geometrical isomerism.

questions.explanation: In 1,1-dichloro-1-pentene, the double-bonded carbon atom is attached to two identical chlorine atoms. For geometrical isomerism to occur, each carbon of the double bond must have two different groups, allowing for different spatial arrangements. In contrast, the other compounds have different groups attached to the double-bonded carbons, permitting restricted rotation and potential geometrical isomers.

questions.text: Identify the compound that does not exhibits tautomerism:-
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 2-Pentanone has a carbonyl group, which can participate in keto-enol tautomerism through proton transfer.
Phenol can exhibit tautomerism through the migration of a hydrogen atom from the hydroxyl group to the ortho or para position on the aromatic ring.
2-Butene does not have the functional groups necessary for tautomerism. It exhibits geometrical isomerism due to restricted rotation around the double bond.
Lactic acid contains both a hydroxyl and a carbonyl group, allowing it to participate in keto-enol tautomerism.

questions.explanation: Tautomerism is a type of isomerism where compounds exist in two interconvertible structural forms due to the migration of a proton. For a compound to exhibit tautomerism, it must have at least one acidic hydrogen atom adjacent to a functional group, such as a carbonyl group, which can facilitate this proton transfer.2-Butene does not have the structural features necessary for tautomerism. It has a double bond, but this only allows for geometrical isomerism (cis-trans isomerism), not tautomerism, because there is no suitable hydrogen migration or functional group interaction. Therefore, the correct answer is 2-Butene.2-Pentanone, Phenol, and Lactic acid all have functional groups that can participate in tautomerism. 2-Pentanone can undergo keto-enol tautomerism due to its carbonyl group. Phenol can tautomerize through hydrogen migration on the aromatic ring, and Lactic acid can undergo keto-enol tautomerism due to the presence of both carbonyl and hydroxyl groups.

questions.text: The minimum number of carbon atoms present in an organic compound to be able to show position isomerism is_
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: With only 2 carbon atoms, there is no possibility of varying the position of a functional group or double bond, which is essential for position isomerism.
Three carbon atoms allow for some variability in the positioning of functional groups or double bonds, which is sufficient for position isomerism.
Though 4 carbon atoms can show position isomerism, the minimum requirement is already satisfied with 3 carbon atoms.
While 5 carbon atoms can certainly exhibit position isomerism, the minimum number required is less than this.

questions.explanation: Position isomerism occurs when there is a change in the position of a functional group, double bond, or other substituents on the carbon chain. This isomerism is possible with a minimum of 3 carbon atoms, as they provide enough structure to allow for different positioning of a functional group or double bond, such as in propanol or propene. Option B is correct because it accurately represents the minimum requirement. Option A is incorrect because 2 carbon atoms do not provide enough structure for position isomerism. Options C and D, while they can show position isomerism, are not the minimum requirement, which is satisfied by 3 carbon atoms.

questions.text: The minimum number of carbon atoms present in an organic compound to show chain isomerism is_
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: With only 2 carbon atoms, such as in ethane, there is no possibility to rearrange the carbon skeleton to form isomers.
With 3 carbon atoms, like in propane, there is still no possibility for chain isomerism as the carbon atoms can only be arranged in a single linear form.
With 4 carbon atoms, such as in butane, chain isomerism is possible because the carbon skeleton can be rearranged into different structures, such as n-butane and isobutane.
While 5 carbon atoms can certainly form chain isomers, it is not the minimum number required. Compounds like pentane can exhibit chain isomerism, but so can butane with 4 carbon atoms.

questions.explanation: In organic chemistry, chain isomerism occurs when compounds with the same molecular formula have different arrangements of the carbon skeleton. For chain isomerism to occur, a compound must have at least 4 carbon atoms. With 4 carbon atoms, distinct structural arrangements such as n-butane and isobutane can be formed, demonstrating chain isomerism. Options with fewer carbon atoms, such as 2 or 3, do not allow for such variability in structure, while 5 carbon atoms also allow chain isomerism but are not the minimum required, thus making option C the correct answer.

questions.text: Which of the following alkenes is the most reactive towards the addition of bromine?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. 2-Butene is a disubstituted alkene, which makes it more stable and less reactive towards bromine compared to less substituted alkenes.
This option is correct. Ethene is a monosubstituted alkene, making it more reactive towards bromine due to less steric hindrance and greater availability of the double bond for addition reactions.
This option is incorrect. 2-Methyl-2-butene is a tetrasubstituted alkene, which is highly stable and therefore less reactive towards bromine compared to less substituted alkenes.

This option is incorrect. Propene is a monosubstituted alkene, but it is still less reactive than ethene due to additional steric hindrance from the methyl group compared to ethene's simpler structure.

questions.explanation: The reactivity of alkenes towards electrophilic addition generally decreases as substitution increases. Ethene is the least substituted alkene among the choices, making its double bond more exposed and reactive towards bromine.

questions.text: The dehydrohalogenation of 2-bromobutane with alcoholic KOH gives:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Both 2-butene and 1-butene are formed, but 2-butene is the major product due to its higher stability.
Both 2-butene and 1-butene are formed, but 2-butene is the major product due to its higher stability.
2-butene is the major product because the more substituted alkene is more stable. The hydrogen is removed from the more substituted carbon, leading to the formation of 2-butene.
1-butene is the minor product because the less substituted alkene is less stable. 2-butene, the more substituted alkene, is the major product due to its higher stability.

questions.explanation: In the dehydrohalogenation of 2-bromobutane with alcoholic KOH, the elimination reaction follows Zaitsev's rule, where the more substituted alkene is more stable and formed preferentially. In this case, 2-butene is more substituted than 1-butene, making it the major product. The third carbon's hydrogen is removed to form 2-butene, which is more stable. Options suggesting only one product or 1-butene as the major product are incorrect due to the stability of 2-butene.

questions.text: Consider the following reactions:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: But-1-yne is a terminal alkyne that reacts with ammonical AgNO3 to form a white precipitate, indicating the presence of a terminal triple bond. This is the correct option.
But-1-ene is an alkene and does not react with ammonical AgNO3 to form a white precipitate, as it's not a terminal alkyne.
But-2-yne is an internal alkyne and does not react with ammonical AgNO3 to form a white precipitate, as it lacks a terminal hydrogen.
But-2-ene is an alkene and does not react with ammonical AgNO3 to form a white precipitate, as it's not a terminal alkyne.

questions.explanation: Ammonical AgNO3 forms a white precipitate with terminal alkynes due to the formation of a silver acetylide, which is a characteristic of terminal alkynes. But-1-yne is the only terminal alkyne among the options and reacts with AgNO3 to form a white precipitate. The other options, But-1-ene and But-2-ene, are alkenes and do not exhibit this reaction. But-2-yne is an internal alkyne and does not react with AgNO3 to form a precipitate due to the lack of a terminal hydrogen.

questions.text: In the presence of peroxide, hydrogen chloride and hydrogen iodide do not give anti-Markovnikov's addition to alkenes because:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is misleading. While HCl and HI exhibit ionic character, this property does not dictate the regioselectivity in radical addition reactions. The mechanism's preference for Markovnikov or anti-Markovnikov addition depends more on the energy profile of the reaction rather than ionic nature.

options.text: One is oxidizing and the other is reducing
options.explanation: This statement is partially true, as HI is indeed a stronger reducing agent. However, the oxidizing or reducing nature of these acids does not explain the lack of anti-Markovnikov addition when peroxides are present, as the mechanism involves radical formation and subsequent addition rather than simple oxidation-reduction reactions.

options.text: One of the steps in the free radical mechanism is endothermic
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. The initiation step of the reaction, which involves the homolytic cleavage of the peroxide bond to generate radicals, is endothermic. This energy requirement creates a barrier that affects the feasibility of the radical addition pathway, thus influencing the regioselectivity toward Markovnikov addition.

options.text: All the steps are exothermic in both cases
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The initiation step in the radical mechanism is endothermic, necessitating energy to break the peroxide bond. As a result, not all steps can be classified as exothermic, which contradicts the fundamental nature of the reaction pathways in this context.

questions.explanation: The anti-Markovnikov addition proceeds via a fraa-radical mechanism. For HCI and Hl, one of the chain propagation steps is highly endothermic and thus energetically unfavorable. This prevents the free-radical chain reaction. from proceeding effectively.

questions.text: What will be formed in the following Kolbe's electrolysis of an aqueous solution of potassium maleate ?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ethene is an alkene that cannot be formed from the dimerization of radicals produced in Kolbe's electrolysis of potassium maleate.
Ethane is a saturated hydrocarbon and cannot be the product of Kolbe's electrolysis, as this process typically yields unsaturated compounds like alkynes.
Ethyne is the correct product formed in this reaction, resulting from the coupling of radicals generated from the decarboxylation of potassium maleate during Kolbe's electrolysis.
Methane is not formed in this reaction because it is a one-carbon alkane, whereas Kolbe's electrolysis of potassium maleate produces higher carbon chain products like ethyne.

questions.explanation: Kolbe's electrolysis of the salt of an unsaturated dicarboxylic acid like maleic acid involves decarboxylation and radical formation at the double bond. The resulting radicals combine to form a triple bond, yielding ethyne (acetylene).

questions.text: Which of the following is not correctly matched?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Hydrolysis of phenyl magnesium iodide - phenol
options.explanation: Phenyl magnesium iodide reacts with water.The phenyl group (C6​H5−​) is protonated to form benzene (C6​H6​).The remaining magnesium iodide hydroxide (Mg(OH)I) is a magnesium salt that is typically a byproduct.
This option correctly identifies γ-isomer of benzene hexachloride (BHC) as lindane, which is indeed the gamma isomer of BHC.
This option accurately relates the (4n+2)π rule with aromaticity, a fundamental concept in determining aromatic compounds in organic chemistry.
This option correctly states that the trimerization of propyne leads to the formation of mesitylene, a process that aligns with known chemical reactions.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A: The hydrolysis of phenyl magnesium iodide does not produce phenol it's yield benzene making this option incorrectly matched. Option B is correctly matched, as the γ-isomer of BHC is indeed lindane. Option C correctly associates the (4n+2)π rule with aromaticity, a rule that helps identify aromatic compounds. Lastly, Option D is correctly matched because the trimerization of propyne leads to mesitylene.

questions.text: Which among the following gives ppt. with terminal alkynyl hydrogen?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ammoniacal silver nitrate reacts with terminal alkynes to form a white precipitate due to the acidic nature of the terminal hydrogen, forming a silver acetylide complex.
Ammoniacal cuprous chloride reacts with terminal alkynes to form a red precipitate due to the acidic nature of the terminal hydrogen, forming a copper acetylide complex.

Both ammoniacal silver nitrate and ammoniacal cuprous chloride react with terminal alkynes. The former forms a white precipitate, while the latter forms a red precipitate, indicating the presence of terminal alkynyl hydrogen.
This option is incorrect because both ammoniacal silver nitrate and ammoniacal cuprous chloride react with terminal alkynes to form precipitates, making 'Both A and B' the correct answer.

questions.explanation: Terminal alkynes have a slightly acidic hydrogen on the triple-bonded carbon. They react with ammoniacal silver nitrate to form silver acetylides with ammoniacal cuprous chloride to form copper(I) acetylides, both giving precipitates. Thus, both reagents can detect terminal alkynes.

questions.text: For preparing monoalkyl benzene, acylation process is preferred rather than direct alkylation because:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: In alkylation, a gaseous byproduct is evolved
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the main issue associated with direct alkylation is the formation of polyalkylated products, rather than the release of a gaseous byproduct.

options.text: In alkylation, a large amount of heat is evolved
options.explanation: This option is misleading; while alkylation can be exothermic, the primary reason for preferring acylation over alkylation is related to product selectivity and the formation of undesired polyalkylated products.

options.text: In alkylation, polyalkylated products are often formed
options.explanation: This is the correct option because the main concern with direct alkylation is the tendency to produce multiple alkyl groups on the benzene ring, leading to polyalkylated products. Acylation, followed by reduction, allows for better control and selectivity in obtaining monoalkyl benzene.

This option is incorrect as cost is not the primary factor influencing the preference for acylation over alkylation. The main issue is the selectivity of the product, particularly the undesired formation of polyalkylated products.

questions.explanation: Friedel-Crafts alkylation is prone to two major side reactions: carbocation rearrangements and polyalkylation. Polyalkylation occurs because the first alkyl group added activates the benzene ring, making it more reactive than the starting material and leading to further alkylation.

questions.text: Which statement is incorrect?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: One of the products exists as three stereoisomers
options.explanation: This statement is correct because a single compound can indeed exist in three stereoisomeric forms, including different enantiomers and a meso form.

options.text: Two pairs of diastereomers are obtained only
options.explanation: This statement is correct as diastereomers are non-mirror image stereoisomers, and it's possible to have multiple pairs depending on the compound's stereochemistry.

options.text: Only one meso stereoisomer is obtained
options.explanation: This statement is correct because a meso compound is an achiral member of a set of stereoisomers, and for some compounds, only one meso form is possible.

options.text: Two pairs of enantiomers are obtained
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect because enantiomers are mirror images and a pair of enantiomers consists of only two compounds. Generally, obtaining two pairs of enantiomers from a single reaction or product is not possible.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D: Two pairs of enantiomers are obtained because enantiomers come in pairs and a single compound cannot form two distinct pairs of enantiomers. Option A is correct as a compound can have three stereoisomers when considering both enantiomers and meso forms. Option B is correct because multiple pairs of diastereomers can exist depending on the stereochemistry involved. Option C is correct because a compound may only have one meso form, which is achiral despite having chiral centers.

questions.text: When isobutane is monochlorinated in the presence of ultra-violet light, the product obtained in higher yield is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: n-butyl chloride cannot be formed from isobutane, as the carbon skeleton of isobutane does not allow for the formation of a straight-chain product like n-butyl chloride.
While isobutyl chloride is a possible product, it is less stable than tert-butyl chloride due to the nature of the carbocation formed, making it a minor product in this reaction.
Sec-butyl chloride cannot be generated from isobutane, as the required structural rearrangement does not occur in this radical substitution reaction.
tert-butyl chloride is the major product due to the stability of the tert-butyl carbocation intermediate, which is favored over the other possible products, leading to its preferential formation.

questions.explanation: Free-radical chlorination of alkanes favors the formation of the most stable radical intermediate. The order of stability for free radicals is tertiary > secondary > primary. Abstraction of the tertiary hydrogen from isobutane leads to the more stable tertiary radical and thus the major product, tert-butyl chloride.

questions.text: Which one of the following alkenes will react fastest with H2 under catalytic hydrogenation condition?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 1-Butene, with its terminal double bond, has the least steric hindrance compared to other more substituted alkenes, allowing it to react faster under catalytic hydrogenation conditions.
2-Methylpropene has increased steric hindrance due to two alkyl groups on the same carbon atom, resulting in slower reaction compared to 1-butene.
2,3-Dimethyl-2-butene is highly substituted and exhibits significant steric hindrance, leading to a slower reaction compared to less substituted alkenes like 1-butene.
2,3-Dimethyl-1-butene, while less hindered than 2,3-dimethyl-2-butene, still experiences higher steric hindrance due to its alkyl groups, resulting in a slower reaction compared to 1-butene.

questions.explanation: The reactivity of alkenes in catalytic hydrogenation is influenced by steric hindrance, with alkenes having less hindrance reacting faster. In this case, 1-Butene reacts the fastest due to its terminal double bond providing the least steric hindrance. 2-Methylpropene is slower due to increased hindrance from alkyl groups on the same carbon. 2,3-Dimethyl-2-butene and 2,3-Dimethyl-1-butene exhibit even more hindrance from greater substitution, resulting in slower reactions compared to 1-butene.

questions.text: Consider the following reaction:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Option A: Primary carbon attacked by Br0 free radical
options.explanation: Attack of Br0 free radical on a primary carbon is less stable compared to a tertiary carbon.

options.text: Option B: Tertiary carbon attacked by Br0 free radical
options.explanation: Attack of Br0 free radical on tertiary carbon is thermodynamically more favored, leading to the formation of a stable tertiary free radical, which is stabilized by hyperconjugation.

options.text: Option C: Secondary carbon attacked by Br0 free radical
options.explanation: Attack of Br0 free radical on secondary carbon is less stable compared to tertiary carbon, though more stable than primary.

options.text: Option D: Quaternary carbon attacked by Br0 free radical
options.explanation: Br0 free radical cannot attack a quaternary carbon since there is no hydrogen to abstract.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B. The attack of the Br0 free radical on a tertiary carbon is thermodynamically more favored because it results in the formation of a tertiary free radical. This free radical is more stable due to hyperconjugation and the inductive effect of surrounding carbon atoms. Option A is incorrect because a primary carbon forms the least stable free radical. Option C is incorrect because, although a secondary carbon free radical is more stable than a primary, it is less stable than a tertiary free radical. Option D is incorrect because a quaternary carbon lacks a hydrogen atom for the Br0 free radical to abstract.

questions.text: The test for unsaturation of organic compounds is carried out by treating alkenes with 1% dilute alkaline KMnO4 solution. The colour of KMnO4 is discharged with the formation of
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ethylene glycol, with the chemical formula HO-CH2-CH2-OH, is a common diol but not the specific type of diol formed in this reaction. The reaction of alkenes with KMnO4 leads to vicinal diols, not ethylene glycol.

The reaction of alkenes with dilute alkaline KMnO4 results in the formation of vicinal diols. The alkene double bond is broken, and hydroxyl groups (-OH) are added to adjacent carbon atoms, forming vicinal glycol.
Glyoxal, with the formula OHCCHO, is an aldehyde, not a diol. This reaction does not produce aldehydes; it specifically forms vicinal diols from alkenes.
Oxalic acid, (COOH)2, is a carboxylic acid, not a diol. The reaction of alkenes with KMnO4 produces vicinal diols, not carboxylic acids.

questions.explanation: When alkenes are treated with 1% dilute alkaline KMnO4, the double bond is broken, and hydroxyl groups (-OH) are added to adjacent carbon atoms, forming vicinal diols. This reaction is a test for unsaturation because it indicates the presence of carbon-carbon double bonds, and the color of the KMnO4 solution is discharged as the permanganate ion is reduced. The correct answer is 'Vicinal glycol'.Ethylene glycol is incorrect because it is not the specific diol formed in this reaction. Glyoxal is incorrect because it is an aldehyde, not a diol. Oxalic acid is incorrect because it is a carboxylic acid, not a diol.

questions.text: Which of the following is produced by the action of CH3CI on benzene in presence of AICI3 followed by oxidation with acidified KMnO4 ?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Toluene is formed in the initial step where CH3Cl reacts with benzene in the presence of AlCl3 as a catalyst.
o-Xylene is not formed in this reaction as the conditions and reagents do not support the formation of a dimethylbenzene isomer.
m-Xylene is not a product of this sequence of reactions because there is no evidence of multiple methyl groups being added to the benzene ring.
Benzoic acid is the final product after the oxidation of toluene with acidified KMnO4, which converts the methyl group to a carboxylic acid group.

questions.explanation: The first step is a Friedel-Crafts alkylation, which produces toluene. The second step is the strong oxidation of the alkyl side chain of toluene. Acidified KMnO4 will oxidize the methyl group of toluene all the way to a carboxylic acid group, forming benzoic acid.

questions.text: Benzoic acid is the only oxidation product along with formation H2O and CO2, of an organic compound A, when A is treated with acidified potassium dichromate, A is
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ethyl benzene, when oxidized using acidified potassium dichromate, forms benzoic acid. The ethyl side chain is completely oxidized to a carboxyl group, resulting in the formation of benzoic acid.
Benzoyl halide does not oxidize to form benzoic acid under these conditions. Instead, it is more likely to undergo hydrolysis or other reactions depending on the halide present.
Aniline is an aromatic amine and does not oxidize to form benzoic acid. Instead, it can undergo oxidation to form different nitrogen-containing compounds.
Benzophenone, a ketone, does not oxidize to form benzoic acid. It is resistant to oxidation under the conditions given.

questions.explanation: When Ethyl benzene is treated with acidified potassium dichromate, the side chain (ethyl group) is oxidized to a carboxylic acid group (-COOH), forming benzoic acid. This is because the strong oxidizing agent fully oxidizes the alkyl side chain to the carboxylic acid. None of the other options, including Benzoyl halide, Aniline, and Benzophenone, undergo oxidation to form benzoic acid under these conditions. Benzoyl halide might undergo hydrolysis, Aniline oxidizes differently due to its amino group, and Benzophenone is resistant to oxidation as it is already a ketone.

questions.text: Nitrobenzene may be prepared by reacting benzene with a mixture of conc. H2SO4 and conc. HNO3 at 55°C. Which of the following best explains the role of conc. H2SO4?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The role of conc. H2SO4 in the preparation of nitrobenzene is to protonate nitric acid, not to remove water. Concentrated sulfuric acid plays a different role in this reaction.

options.text: It form stable complex with benzene
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The role of conc. H2SO4 in the preparation of nitrobenzene is to protonate nitric acid, not to form a stable complex with benzene. The interaction between concentrated sulfuric acid and nitric acid is essential in this reaction.

This option is correct. The role of conc. H2SO4 in the preparation of nitrobenzene is to protonate nitric acid. When nitric acid is protonated by sulfuric acid, it forms a nitronium ion that reacts with benzene to produce nitrobenzene.

This option is incorrect. The role of conc. H2SO4 in the preparation of nitrobenzene is to protonate nitric acid, not to act as a solvent. Concentrated sulfuric acid serves a specific function in the reaction mechanism, different from acting as a solvent.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C: It is protonating nitric acid. Concentrated sulfuric acid plays a crucial role in protonating nitric acid, which leads to the formation of a nitronium ion that reacts with benzene to yield nitrobenzene.
